title: practical english usage - Part 267
content:
but Americans do not say half after. And in American English of before and till In American English after is often used instead of past (e.g. ten after six); vocabulary •  326   names and titles: Daniel; Mr Lewis Can I help you, madam?

(e.g. shop assistants).

Sir and madam are used in Britain mostly by people in service occupations not                                   or Excuse me. Can you tell me the time? (     Excuse me, Mr-.     Excuse me, Mrs.) Mr, Mrs and Ms are not generally used alone.

alone (e.g.  'Hello, Smith'), but this is unusual in modern English.

Members of all-male groups sometimes address each other by their surnames for example.

person that we are talking to. It would be unusual to say 'Hello, Peter Matthews', Note that we  do not usually use both the first name and the surname of a Good morning Miss Williamson.

This is more formal or respectful.

We can offer you 5%  interest guaranteed for 3 years...

advertising literature and similar correspondence.

However, it is now becoming common for first names to be used by strangers in Hello, Olivia. How are you?  Hi, Dan. Did you get my last email?

This is informal, used for example to colleagues, relatives, friends and children.

When we  talk (or write) to people we  generally name them in one of two ways.

2 talking     to  people Let's put Billows in goal and move Carter up.

groups like soldiers or team members) when they refer to each other.

Surnames alone are sometimes used by members of groups (especially all-male Thatcher was the first British woman Prime Minister.

The 5,000 metres was won by Jones. I never liked Eliot's poetry.

Do you think Roberts would make a good President?

politicians, sports personalities, writers and so on.

We   often use just the surname to talk about men and women in public life - d  surname only alone (Mr John).

surname (e.g. Mr John Parker). Mr, Mrs, etc are not used before the first name Note that it is less usual to talk about people by using Mr, Mrs, etc + first name + There's a Ms Sanders on the phone.

Ask Miss Andrews to come in, please.

We've got a new teacher called Mrs Campbell.

Can I speak to Mr Lewis, please?

want to show respect or be polite.

This is more formal. We   talk like this about people we  do not know, or when we c   title (Mr, Mrs. etc) + surname Vocabulary Areas  Section 29 vocabulary' •  327   names: Florence, Homer, etc in English.

The  Italian artists Raffaello Sanzio and Tiziano are called Raphael and Titian 3 artists Sophocles, Mercury, Jupiter, Helen, Troy, Odysseus Homer, Aeschylus, Livy, Horace, Ovid, Virgil, Aesop, Aristotle, Euclid, The  same is true of many classical Greek and Roman names. Some examples: 2 classical      names Turin, Venice, Vienna, Warsaw (now usually Beijing), Prague, Rome, St Petersburg Seville, Thessalonica, usually Marseille), Milan, Moscow, Munich,  Naples, Oporto, Padua, Peking Livorno), Lisbon, Lyons (now more usually Lyon), Marseilles (now more Geneva, Genoa, The Hague, Hanover, Jerusalem, Leghorn (now more usually Bucharest, Calcutta (now usually Kolkata), Cologne, Damascus, Florence, Antwerp, Athens, Beirut, Belgrade, Bombay (now usually Mumbai), Brussels, names for cities: in French, and Copenhagen in Italian and English. Some examples of English capital of Denmark, Kobenhavn, is called Kopenhagen in German, Copenhague The  names of cities are often different in different languages - for example the 1  cities 327 names:    Florence,        Homer,      etc For ways of addressing people in letters, ►    2B9. Note that we  do not normally combine two titles such as Prof Dr or Mrs Dr.

teachers (especially, in Britain, for more senior university teachers).

Professor does not simply mean ‘ teacher’ . It is a title reserved for university Dr (= Doctor) is used as a title for medical and other doctors.

use Ms before their own names in preference to Mrs or Miss.

(or has no reason to say) whether they are married. Many women also choose to especially in writing, to talk about or address women when one does not know Like Mr, Ms does not show whether somebody is married or not. It is often used, Mr (= Mister) is not normally written in full, and the other two cannot be.
